dataName: sargassum muticum japanese wireweed sargassum algae
region: europe atlantic north-sea baltic mediterranean americas na-pacific
name: Japanese Wireweed
description: Invasive brown algae that forms dense floating mats and attaches strongly to hard surfaces like hulls.
features: Brown/yellowish algae
Small air vesicles (look like berries)
Can grow up to several meters long

region: europe atlantic mediterranean north-sea oceania americas na-pacific sa-atlantic
name: Wakame
description: Marine algae listed as invasive globally. Grows rapidly on hulls and port infrastructure.
features: Large brown fronds
Distinct central vein (midrib)
Very wavy and frilly base (sporophyll)

name: Star Ascidian
description: Colonial invertebrate that encrusts on hulls, creating gelatinous mats that smother other species.
features: Colonies form star-shaped patterns
Gelatinous/rubbery texture
Various colors (orange, yellow, green, purple)

region: europe atlantic mediterranean black-sea americas gulf-mexico
name: Striped Barnacle
description: Warm-water crustacean. Causes huge hydrodynamic drag on ships and spreads rapidly.
features: Calcareous cone shape
Vertical purple or brown stripes/lines
Direct primary fouling on metal/paint

dataName: rugulopteryx okamurae asian brown weed algae
name: Asian Brown Weed
description: A highly aggressive brown algae that covers massive areas of the rocky seabed. Transferred via anchors and fishing gear.
features: Yellowish-brown, flat, ribbon-like branches
Forms extremely dense mats
Attaches strongly to hard surfaces and ropes

dataName: asparagopsis armata harpoon weed algae red
name: Harpoon Weed
description: An aggressive red algae officially listed in European legislation. Travels entangled in hull structures and equipment.
features: Pink to dark red color
Barbed, harpoon-like hooks on branches
Fluffy or feathery appearance underwater

region: europe atlantic north-sea mediterranean americas na-pacific na-atlantic oceania
name: Club Tunicate
description: A filter-feeding sea squirt that creates heavy fouling on hulls and docks, significantly increasing vessel drag.
features: Club or baseball bat shape with a stalk
Leathery, bumpy outer skin
Brownish to yellowish color

dataName: didemnum vexillum carpet sea squirt tunicate
region: europe atlantic north-sea mediterranean americas na-atlantic na-pacific oceania
name: Carpet Sea Squirt
description: A highly invasive colonial tunicate that creates fleshy, mat-like colonies that smother native marine life.
features: Pale orange, cream, or off-white color
Looks like dripping wax or vomit
Leathery, veiny surface

region: europe north-sea baltic atlantic americas na-atlantic na-pacific
name: Chinese Mitten Crab
description: Invasive crustacean that travels hidden in crevices or in ballast water. Migrates between the sea and freshwater estuaries.
features: Claws covered in dense 'wool' or 'hair'
Olive-green to brown carapace

region: europe baltic north-sea atlantic mediterranean black-sea americas na-pacific
name: Soft-shell Clam
description: Bivalve mollusc frequently found in sediments that accumulate at the bottom of sea chests of ships stationary for a long time.
features: Oval and fragile (chalky) shell
Long fleshy siphon (tube)

name: Asian Shore Crab
description: A highly invasive crab replacing native species. Often transported in sea chests and ballast water.
features: Square-shaped carapace
Claws with red/blood-like spots

name: Sea Walnut
description: A devastating comb jelly transported in ballast water. It caused the collapse of fisheries in the Black Sea.
features: Transparent, walnut-shaped body
Lobed structure (no tentacles)
Rows of cilia that diffract light (rainbow effect)

name: Atlantic Blue Crab
description: A voracious predator, originally from the Americas, now devastating bivalve populations in the Mediterranean.
features: Bright blue claws
Olive green carapace with sharp lateral spines

dataName: rapana venosa veined rapa whelk snail
region: europe black-sea mediterranean atlantic americas na-atlantic sa-atlantic
name: Veined Rapa Whelk
description: A large predatory sea snail that feeds on native oysters and mussels. Larvae spread via ballast water.
features: Heavy, rounded shell (up to 18 cm)
Bright orange coloring on the inside of the shell opening

name: Striped Eel Catfish
description: The only marine fish on the EU list of invasive alien species. Highly venomous and dangerous to divers.
features: Eel-like body with four pairs of barbels around the mouth
Brown-black body with two white/yellow horizontal stripes

name: Asian Green Mussel
description: A large, fast-growing mussel that severely fouls hulls, sea chests, and intake pipes throughout the Caribbean, Gulf of Mexico, and South America.
features: Vivid green to dark green outer shell edge
Smooth, elongated, and teardrop-shaped
Can grow up to 16 cm (6 inches) long
Forms extremely dense clusters on hard structures

region: americas na-atlantic na-pacific sa-atlantic oceania east-asia
name: European Green Crab
description: Listed as one of the world's 100 worst invasive species. It travels globally via ballast water and sea chests, devastating native bivalve populations.
features: Five distinct spines on the outside edge of each eye
Carapace is broader than it is long
Color ranges from dark green to brown or reddish patches
Highly aggressive when approached

name: Zebra Mussel
description: A freshwater and estuarine bivalve introduced via ballast water. It causes billions of dollars in damage by clogging cooling systems.
features: Small, D-shaped shell
Distinctive zigzag or striped black-and-white pattern
Sits flat on its underside
Typically very small (the size of a fingernail)

name: Orange Cup Coral
description: An invasive stony coral that heavily fouls ship hulls and oil rigs in the Gulf of Mexico and Brazil, outcompeting native sponges and corals.
features: Bright orange, red, or yellow polyps
Forms cup-like skeletal structures
Does not rely on sunlight (can grow in dark sea chests)
Extends bright yellow tentacles at night to feed

dataName: ciona intestinalis vase tunicate sea squirt
name: Vase Tunicate
description: A solitary sea squirt that creates massive biofouling weight on vessel hulls and aquaculture gear across the Pacific and Oceania.
features: Tubular, translucent, jelly-like body
Yellow or green tint at the tips of its siphons
Grows densely packed together like tubes
Can squirt water when squeezed or removed

region: americas na-atlantic na-pacific europe atlantic north-sea
name: Skeleton Shrimp
description: A highly invasive amphipod that travels in secondary biofouling (living on algae that grows on hulls). Extremely prevalent in US and European ports.
features: Looks like an underwater praying mantis or stick insect
Thread-like body, often red or brown
Clings to algae, ropes, and buoys in massive swarms
Males have large, fuzzy claws

region: indo-pacific east-asia southeast-asia south-asia indian-ocean oceania
name: Black-striped Mussel
description: A highly destructive marine and brackish bivalve. It forms massive, dense layers on ship hulls, sea water piping, and port infrastructure.
features: Small shell (up to 2.5 cm)
Dark brown to black stripes or completely black/dark brown
Very similar to the Zebra Mussel, but lives in marine/estuarine environments
Grows in exceptionally tight, multi-layered clusters

region: americas na-pacific sa-pacific na-atlantic sa-atlantic gulf-mexico caribbean oceania europe mediterranean
name: Red Rust Bryozoan
description: A critical biofouling threat because it is highly resistant to copper-based anti-fouling paints. Once it attaches to a hull, it provides a safe surface for other invasive species to settle on.
features: Forms flat, crusty, or leafy colonies
Striking bright red, orange, or rust-black color
Hard, brittle texture like a potato chip
Often found directly on anti-fouling paint

name: Red Lionfish
description: One of the most devastating invasive fish in the Atlantic and Caribbean. While introduced via aquarium trade/ballast, port divers frequently encounter them hiding in sea chests.
features: Distinctive red, maroon, and white vertical stripes
Large, fan-like pectoral fins
Long, separated dorsal spines containing potent venom
Extremely dangerous to handle

region: americas na-atlantic sa-atlantic na-pacific europe atlantic north-sea mediterranean
name: Australian Tubeworm
description: A reef-building tubeworm that thrives in ports and estuaries. It rapidly encrusts hulls and propellers with heavy, rock-hard calcium carbonate tubes.
features: Builds intertwining, white calcareous (chalky) tubes
Tubes are typically 1-2 mm wide but form massive reefs
Worm has a feathery, brown/green crown when extended
Leaves sharp, razor-like crusts when scraped off

region: americas caribbean gulf-mexico sa-atlantic na-pacific south-asia southeast-asia indian-ocean
name: Snowflake Coral
description: A highly invasive soft coral that grows rapidly on shaded artificial structures, metal hulls, and oil rigs, completely smothering native black corals.
features: Erect, branching, tree-like stems
Branches are covered in beautiful, white, star-like polyps
Stems are often pale pink or orange beneath the white polyps
Thrives in dark niche areas and under piers

region: americas gulf-mexico caribbean sa-atlantic europe mediterranean
name: Australian Spotted Jellyfish
description: While adults are free-swimming and clog fishing nets, they spread globally because their polyp stage attaches to ship hulls like biofouling.
features: Large, brownish bell covered in bright white spots
Chunky, cauliflower-like oral arms instead of trailing tentacles
Can grow up to 50 cm (20 inches) across
Forms massive swarms in the Gulf of Mexico

region: americas na-atlantic sa-atlantic na-pacific gulf-mexico europe atlantic mediterranean oceania
name: Brown Bryozoan
description: A notorious and widespread macro-fouling organism. It creates immense hydrodynamic drag on vessels and easily transports across oceans on hull surfaces.
features: Looks like a bushy, dark purple or reddish-brown seaweed
Actually an animal colony, not a plant
Grows in clumps up to 8-10 cm high
Feels slightly stiff or crispy compared to soft algae

name: Killer Algae
description: A highly toxic, aggressive green algae that can clone itself from tiny fragments left on anchors or hull crevices. Eradicated in California but remains a strict watch species.
features: Bright, neon green color
Looks like underwater fern or flattened feathers
Grows rapidly on a horizontal runner (stolon)
Toxic to most marine herbivores

region: americas na-atlantic sa-atlantic na-pacific gulf-mexico europe atlantic north-sea oceania
name: Orange-striped Green Anemone
description: A tiny but incredibly resilient anemone that thrives on ship hulls and survives extreme temperature and salinity changes during ocean crossings.
features: Small body, rarely larger than 3 cm
Olive green column usually marked with vertical orange or yellow stripes
Up to 100 thin, translucent tentacles
Often found growing among mussels and barnacles on hulls

name: Common Slipper Limpet
description: Introduced from North America, this sea snail is a major pest in Europe. It heavily fouls hulls, sea chests, and completely smothers native oyster beds.
features: Oval, arched shell resembling a slipper or boat
Has a distinctive internal white 'shelf' or deck
Grows in curved, stacked chains (one on top of another)
Color is typically cream or pale brown with reddish streaks

name: New Zealand Barnacle
description: Brought to Europe on ship hulls during WWII, it outcompetes native barnacles. It is a highly aggressive primary fouler on vessels, tolerating brackish water.
features: Has exactly 4 calcareous shell plates (unlike native European barnacles which have 6)
Smooth, grayish-white or brownish cone
Diamond-shaped or cross-shaped opening
Grows very rapidly, creating severe hydrodynamic drag

name: Dark False Mussel
description: A severe biofouling pest in European brackish waters (especially the Baltic Sea). It completely clogs internal cooling pipes of ships and industrial power plants.
features: Small, thumbnail-sized shell (usually under 2 cm)
Dark brown or yellowish-brown, without the zigzag stripes of a Zebra mussel
Has a small 'tooth' (apophysis) inside the hinge of the shell
Forms exceptionally dense, mat-like clusters in pipes

region: europe atlantic north-sea mediterranean americas na-pacific oceania
name: Pacific Oyster
description: Originally introduced for aquaculture, it has escaped and now forms massive, razor-sharp reefs on port infrastructure, ship hulls, and natural shorelines.
features: Large, highly irregular and deeply fluted (wavy) shell
Can grow very large (up to 30 cm / 12 inches)
Edges of the shell are extremely sharp
Purple, brown, or green streaks on a whitish background

name: Grape Caulerpa
description: One of the most invasive seaweeds in the Mediterranean. It forms dense, continuous carpets over the seabed and spreads easily via anchors and hull appendages.
features: Bright green color
Distinctive small, bulbous or grape-like structures along the stems
Grows from a creeping horizontal runner
Releases a toxic chemical that deters native fish from eating it

dataName: lagocephalus sceleratus silver cheeked toadfish pufferfish
name: Silver-cheeked Toadfish
description: Entered the Mediterranean via the Suez Canal. A major threat for divers inspecting hulls or port infrastructure, as it is highly aggressive and extremely toxic.
features: Elongated pufferfish body with a silvery band along the sides
Dark greenish back covered in symmetrical black spots
Contains deadly tetrodotoxin (paralyzing poison)
Has powerful, beak-like teeth capable of biting through dive gear

dataName: asterias amurensis northern pacific seastar starfish
name: Northern Pacific Seastar
description: Native to East Asia, this voracious predator is one of Australia's worst marine pests. Larvae travel in ballast water, while juveniles hide in sea chests and biofouling niches.
features: Yellow, orange, or purple coloring with a distinctly pointed star shape
Five arms with slightly upturned, pointed tips
Can grow up to 50 cm (20 inches) across
Underside is completely yellow

name: Mediterranean Fanworm
description: A massive biofouling issue in Australia and New Zealand. Forms dense canopies on ship hulls, pontoon bottoms, and port infrastructure, completely blocking native marine life.
features: Builds a tough, flexible, leathery tube (up to 80 cm long)
Extends a large, spiral, flower-like crown of feeding tentacles
Crown is usually banded with orange, purple, and white
Quickly retracts into its tube when disturbed or touched

region: indo-pacific east-asia southeast-asia south-asia indian-ocean oceania europe mediterranean
name: Spaghetti Bryozoan
description: A devastating macro-fouling colony that looks like tangled noodles. It causes immense hydrodynamic drag on ship hulls and rapidly clogs intake grates in tropical/subtropical Asian ports.
features: Looks exactly like tangled masses of wet, yellowish-brown spaghetti
Soft, gelatinous, and rubbery texture
Grows exceptionally fast in warm, polluted port waters
Often smothers other biofouling like barnacles and mussels

region: indo-pacific east-asia southeast-asia south-asia indian-ocean oceania
name: Elegant Tubeworm
description: One of the most prolific primary calcareous foulers in tropical Asian ports (like Singapore and Hong Kong). It rapidly encrusts bare metal, propellers, and anti-fouling paint.
features: Forms small, white, twisting chalky tubes (up to 3 cm)
Tubes have prominent ridges along their length
Grows in massive, intertwined, rock-hard crusts
Extends a small crown of tentacles with a spine-covered 'stopper' (operculum)

name: Asian Paddle Crab
description: An aggressive crab native to East Asia that has heavily invaded New Zealand. It travels in ballast water and hides in complex sea chest gratings.
features: Six sharp spines on each side of the carapace (shell)
Hind legs are flattened into distinctive paddles for swimming
Color ranges from pale green to olive brown
Extremely vicious and combative

dataName: megabalanus rosa pink titan acorn barnacle
name: Pink Titan Barnacle
description: A very large, heavy-fouling barnacle native to Japan and East Asia. Its massive size creates extreme drag on commercial vessels operating in Asian trade routes.
features: Very large cone shape (can exceed 5 cm in diameter)
Distinctive pink or pink-red outer shell
Shell surface is smooth, unlike many other large barnacles
Grows aggressively on ship hulls and propeller shafts

dataName: codium fragile dead mans fingers green sea fleece algae
name: Dead Man's Fingers
description: Native to the Northwest Pacific, this heavy green algae easily fragments. Tiny pieces get caught in hull imperfections and anchors, allowing it to spread to Oceania and the rest of the world.
features: Dark green color with a sponge-like, velvety texture
Thick, branching, cylindrical fronds that look like thick fingers
Hangs heavily from port structures and static ship hulls
Can weigh down heavily on aquaculture ropes and grates

region: east-asia oceania americas na-pacific sa-pacific sa-atlantic
name: Mediterranean Mussel
description: Native to Europe, this is a massive biofouling pest in Asia, the Americas, and Oceania. It forms incredibly dense beds that sink buoys and completely cover ship hulls.
features: Smooth, dark blue to black shell, sometimes with brown edges
Pointed, beak-like tip (umbo) that turns slightly downwards
Grows in enormous, tightly packed clusters on vertical surfaces

name: Onyx Slipper Snail
description: Native to Southern California, this snail has heavily invaded Hong Kong, Japan, and Korea. It thrives in highly polluted ports and smothers local hull surfaces.
features: Oval, arched shell with a rich dark brown or black exterior
Internal 'deck' or shelf is brilliant white
Stacks on top of each other just like the Common Slipper Limpet
Very tolerant to low oxygen in dirty port waters

region: east-asia south-asia southeast-asia indian-ocean europe atlantic mediterranean black-sea
name: Ivory Barnacle
description: Native to the Americas, this white barnacle is now a primary fouler in European, Indian, and Asian ports. It rapidly degrades anti-fouling coatings.
features: Pure white, smooth cone-shaped shell
Relatively low and flat compared to other large barnacles
Diamond-shaped opening
Found heavily encrusted on propellers and sea chest grates

name: Pygmy Mussel
description: Originating in Australasia, this tiny mussel has severely invaded Europe and East Asia. It clogs internal ship pipework in brackish water ports.
features: Very small shell (rarely larger than 1.5 cm)
Dark brown to black, shiny surface
Hinge is located strictly at the front edge (terminal umbo)
Tends to mix with native mussels, making identification tricky

name: Brown Mussel
description: Native to Africa and South America, this aggressive mussel causes severe biofouling on offshore oil rigs and vessels in India, the Indian Ocean, and the Gulf of Mexico.
features: Large, elongated shell up to 12 cm long
Brown to yellow-brown color (unlike the green edges of the Asian Green Mussel)
Highly competitive, completely displacing native bivalves on hard surfaces

region: east-asia south-asia americas na-atlantic sa-atlantic oceania
name: European Sea Squirt
description: Native to Europe, this solitary tunicate has invaded the Americas, Asia, and Oceania. It densely fouls aquaculture nets and ship hulls, increasing fuel consumption.
features: Oval, egg-shaped body (usually 5-10 cm long)
Translucent or dirty grey/brown, rough and cartilaginous skin
Two siphons located close to each other at the top
Often covered in mud or small algae
